title: Whittled hazel peg dolls
subtitle: A set of simple peg dolls whittled from hazel branch sections
excerpt: Three small peg dolls whittled from hazel branch sections: a rounded head, a tapered neck, and a cylindrical body. Each doll is 80 to 100 mm tall and takes under an hour to whittle. The bark can be left on the body or removed; the head is carved smooth. A good project for practising the push cut and the stop cut.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the peg doll whittling form; traditional British folk toy making practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Carving a form from a piece of wood using a knife only, without gouges or a mallet. All cuts are push cuts or pull cuts from the hand.
A cut made across the grain at a defined point to prevent a following paring cut from running further than intended. Used to define the neck and base of the peg doll.
A cut where the knife blade is pushed away from the body along the workpiece. The safest and most controlled cut for beginners.
The rough piece of wood from which the final form is carved; here, a hazel branch section cut to 100 mm.

content.text: A peg doll is a classic
whittling
project: a
blank
shaped to suggest the human form without realistic detail. The two key cuts are the
stop cut
that defines the neck, and the
push cuts
that round the head. Hazel branch sections are ideal: the bark on the body gives a natural skirt or trouser suggestion without any additional work, and the peeled head contrasts with it.
Wood

Hazel branch sections 18 to 22 mm in diameter and 90 to 100 mm long. Seasoned hazel (air-dried for six months or more) is easier to control than green hazel; it holds a clean edge without tearing. Select sections without knots, especially in the upper third of the blank where the head will be. Cut the base flat with the saw so the doll stands without a base plate.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Marking the neck

Mark the neck position with a pencil ring around the blank, 25 mm from the top of the blank. This is where the head meets the body. The neck will be narrowed to approximately 12 mm across at this point.
The stop cut
At the neck pencil line, make a
stop cut

all the way around the blank: the knife is pressed straight in, perpendicular to the long axis of the blank, to a depth of 3 to 4 mm. This cut defines the top of the body and prevents the bark-paring pass on the body from running up into the head area.
Peeling the head and neck

Remove the bark from the top 25 mm of the blank using push cuts, working from the top of the blank down toward the neck stop cut. The push cuts peel the bark and the outer few millimetres of sapwood to leave a smooth cylinder. Continue push cuts to reduce the neck area to 12 mm diameter, working from the head section down to the stop cut and from the body section up to it.
Rounding the head
Round the top of the head with a series of light
push cuts

from the flat top surface down the sides. Remove the sharp top corner first, then the high spots that follow, working toward a smooth dome. The goal is not a perfect sphere but a recognisable rounded head. Six to eight push cuts from each angle is usually enough.
Finishing

Sand the head and neck area with 320-grit to smooth any remaining facets. The bark body is left as-is. Apply a very light coat of Danish oil to the head and neck; wipe off immediately. The bark body does not need finishing. Faces can be added with a pyrography tool or a fine permanent pen.
Variations
Fully peeled doll: remove the bark from the whole blank before carving. The resulting doll is paler and more polished-looking.
Apple wood version: apple branch sections have a fine grain and beautiful colour once oiled; they make more refined peg dolls for display rather than play.
Miniature set: a set of five or six matching dolls from a single hazel branch, graduated in size from 60 to 100 mm, makes a good gift or display piece.
